title: Whooping cough (pertussis): Travel health advice - Canada.ca
content:
## Whooping cough (pertussis): Travel health advice
- Symptoms and treatment
- Spread, prevention and risks
- Travel health advice
- For health professionals
Whooping cough remains  a very common infection in many parts of the world. Anyone who isn’t protected against whooping cough is at risk of being  infected with it. This is especially the case when travelling internationally to  destinations with low vaccination coverage. This is especially the case in settings that promote close contact, such as festivals or sporting  events that attract large numbers or people.
Check travel advice  and advisories for your destinations for  information on health risks and recommendations.
Travel  advice and advisories
- Whooping  cough (pertussis): Spread, prevention and risks
Consult a health care provider or  visit a travel health clinic preferably 6 weeks before you travel to get personalized  health advice. Even if your travel date is coming up soon, it's still  worthwhile to make an appointment.
- updating your routine vaccinations if necessary,  including for whooping cough
- any extra medications or  vaccines you may need based on your travel plans
If you get sick  before your trip, consider postponing your travel plans until you've recovered.
- Who is most at risk of  getting whooping cough
Use personal  protective measures to reduce the risk of getting or spreading infectious  respiratory diseases. This can include:
- avoiding close contact  with people who are sick
- wearing a well-fitting  respirator or mask
- cleaning your hands  regularly
- covering your coughs and sneezes
- cleaning and disinfecting high-touch surfaces and  objects
If you have symptoms of whooping  cough or have been exposed to someone who’s ill:
- stay at your accommodation and limit contact with  others
- call a health care provider
- How whooping cough spreads
- Clean your  hands to help reduce the spread of infectious diseases
- Respiratory infectious       diseases: Reduce your risk with personal protective measures
If you develop symptoms of whooping  cough after you return, call a health care provider for advice.
Before an in-person appointment,  tell them:
- where you’ve  been travelling or living
The health care  provider may give you advice on how to prevent spreading the infection to others at the health care  facility.
You can also consider using personal  protective measures if you’re sick when you return to Canada.
- Respiratory infectious       diseases: Reduce your risk with personal protective measures
- Tips for  healthy travel
- Border and  travel health
- Symptoms of whooping  cough
- Vaccines  and medications before travel
- If you  become sick or injured while travelling outside Canada or after your return
